your hand into this and then you get a full wristed motion for this needle holder now I chose not to use a port it's a 6mm calibre hub I think and so I thought it would be easier just to insert directly through the soft tissue certainly in the chest

and it's a ligature and it's a hook so here's the ligature using that and then i can click instantly to a hook just with the flick of a button on the ligature hook so you have the two things you need for a vats thymectomy which is the ability to use energy to divide and here's the ligature and then sometimes you need to pull towards yourself which is why the hook's so useful

to be able to get that grasp, the exact grasp that you wanted and to be able to just lift up and then to divide the tissue.

that reamer vein and we want to dissect out the reamer vein and really that's the far lateral extent of the division of the excision of the thymus so thymectomy for myasthenia gravis it's important to remove as much tissue as possible and in the very recent new england journal medicine randomized study which proved the fantastic benefit of thymectomy versus conservative treatment
They did only use stenotomy because they weren't convinced that at that stage, and the study was set up initially over 10 years ago, that it was possible to get rid of all the tissue by vats.
But I hope you can see in this video that you really can do a fantastic job.

of getting rid of all the fatty tissues whether it's the thymus so now we've clicked from the ligature to the hook and we're just following just medial to the reamer just to dissect the thymus off the back of the sternum

Now we're coursing across the pericardium just above the diaphragm and we're going to go and open the pleura on the other side.

I use CO2 at 8 millimetres of mercury pressure and once I've opened the other side I go back onto two lungs and I guarantee you will not notice the difference once you've gone back onto two lungs you get great ventilation the CO2 doesn't rise and it's never a problem at all I have met some thymectomy surgeons who don't open the pleura I think this is definitely a mistake

If you open the pleura you can get out much more laterally and as you'll see in a bit we're going to put a sub-xiphoid port in so that we can remove the thymus sub-xiphoid but also you can insert the 30 degree camera sub-xiphoid and get a perfect view of the left phrenic which is always quite difficult to see from the right unless you have either a sub-xiphoid port or a port on the opposite side.
So now we're back up to the cervical portion, which I think is always the most difficult.
We're just dissecting out the reamer vein.
I think you should always have a very low threshold for just dividing the reamer vein.
If it's at all in the way, just use the ligature to get rid of it.

So there's a nice view of how the FlexDex is able to articulate out of the way so that we can continue to dissect the thymus off the pericardium.
I have a very low threshold for going into the pericardium also.
Usually, mainly for thymomas, I like to take pericardium with it, because otherwise the resection margin can be quite difficult to prove as R0.
But obviously in a myasthenic patient, this isn't as important.

And we're going to pull this superior horn down and just continue to get it out of the neck.
I think this is the most difficult bit in a VATS thymectomy.
And the most important thing is not to give up, not to start cutting across the fatty tissue, because then you lose all your tissue planes.
You actually make sure you stay above the thymus.
That's where the dissection plane is.
So now this is the left superior horn that we're coming to.
I'm going over the trachea.
I want to see the trachea and I want to have no fatty tissue on the trachea at all.
I want to dissect it out completely.

Of note, I didn't take any instruments out for the whole operation.

You need absolutely no more instruments than that.

Cervical incision also makes it difficult to maintain CO2 in the chest.
And CO2 in the chest really is the most important thing about a thymectomy.
